segments.text: February 14, Valentine's Day, sitting in the basement of the Weissenbaker building,
getting ready to interview Charlie Holland, who got here a little more than 50 years ago.
Charlie, test your mic.
Testing one, two.
Is it all right?
For someone who may not have known you in a few years to come when they're looking at this material or listening to it,
would you begin by summarizing your background and experience, where you grew up, went to school,
your experience before you got to Texas A&M?
Well, I grew up in western North Carolina, and my dad was a Baptist minister.
In fact, I started the first grade the year he finished Wake Forest.
He had been in World War I, and he decided to go down and finish up.
So we moved to Wake Forest, and about Christmas time, the twins were born,
and he took me out.
He took me out of the first grade, because he was afraid I might bring home some childhood diseases.
And then the next year after that, we moved to Mountain View,
and I was just recently at Christmas going up there looking for the place that we moved to.
It was on a dirt road out from North Wilkesboro,
and this will kind of give you the view of what things were like in those times.
And he was pastor of a little Baptist church.
There was a Baptist junior college high school, and he taught the mathematics there.
And this was back in time that the Depression was coming on us out there.
And all he got out of that year's teaching at that little junior college
was an Underwood typewriter, which I have at home.
That was all they could pay.
And I went to school.
It was a little one-room schoolhouse.
First grade in the first row, second grade in the second.
Pot-bellied stove.
Down at the bottom on old slick red hill was an outhouse.
And all the kids went to the outhouse.
And also the water was simply a barrel, just like you'd see a keg of beer.
And so Dad said, when we went there, I better put you back in the first grade,
because I'm afraid I don't want you to get behind.
So I started out in the first.
Well, in that school, you could get promoted.
All you had to do was just show you could do the second grade work.
So before that...
The year was over.
I was over in the second grade, second row.
They'd moved me to the second.
So you just changed seats.
You didn't know...
No paperwork.
Before the end of the year, I was in the third row.
Well, then we moved to Stony Point, which is about 60 miles from Charlotte.
And it's still very primitive in those days,
but it did have a consolidated school.
They bused all the kids from the county into that school.
So Dad said, well, I know you were in the third.
You were in the third grade there, but I think I'll put you back and let you do the second over.
I don't want you to get behind.
Well, they put me in that second grade, and I stayed the whole year,
because they didn't know how to get me out of it, even though I knew it.
Well, right after Christmas, we went back there and was looking for this.
We wanted to go to all the places that we'd lived.
And so we went to North Wilkesboro, and there was a bypass, and we stopped at a McDonald's.
And this Mountain View wasn't even on the map yet.
And so we went there to stop at a McDonald's.
And I saw a well-dressed lady over there.
And I went over and struck up a conversation with her.
And I asked her if she knew where Mountain View was.
She said, oh, it's just right up here.
You turn off, right up the road here.
Now, let me point out that in those years,
Wilkes County was noted to be the biggest white lightning county in the United States.
And so she said, yes.
I said, I went to school there, one-room schoolhouse, outhouse down the bottom.
I went to school in the middle of the mountain hill.
And so then I went on back and sat down.
And she came back over afterwards, and she told my daughter, or my sister,
that her husband had passed away.
And she says, well, what did he do?
She says, oh, he was a bootlegger.
That was the only way to make a living in those years.
She said, well, didn't the law get after him?
She said, oh, yes, he built time five different times.
So this was kind of the, you know,
the area that I grew up in.
And my dad and I, he was always gone a lot and so forth.
And we just didn't have these father-to-son talks that you always have nowadays
or hear of, like Andy Hardy and Judge Stone.
The closest we ever came to it was the day we were packing the car.
He's getting ready to take me off to this little Baptist Junior College,
Mars Hill, out from Nashville, North Carolina.
And it was always assumed that I'd go to college.
It was never even, we just didn't discuss it.
And I delivered papers there, and I knew all the professional people in town.
And they said, you've got, there's a lot of emphasis there on you need to,
you need to go to college and make something yourself.
You heard that over and over again.
And so as we were packing the car, Dad said to me, he says,
son, what are you going to study when you get up there?
I said, well,
Dad, I thought I'd study dentistry.
He said, no, son, I don't think I'd do that if I were you.
I don't believe you'll ever be able to keep up with your tools.
Well, see, I'd lost his saw and hammer so many times that he was just,
and he said, why don't you just go ahead and study engineering?
Well, that's what I did.
And so after the pre-engineering course up there,
Mars Hill, I went to North Carolina State.
And my junior year, I was having breakfast, and that's when Pearl Harbor hit.
Actually, the day we were packing the car,
the Germans marched in Europe, which marked the beginning,
which is the day I started to college.
And then when I was in my junior year at North Carolina State,
the Germans, the Pearl Harbor occurred.
So I went down, and I didn't want to get drafted.
I wanted to finish school.
So what I did was they had a program there where you could become,
get a direct commission, even though you hadn't been in ROTC.
They had an ROTC there, and people marched around all the time.
And they told me I didn't need one point in taking.
I didn't need one point in it, because I could only do two years of it.
So I didn't take it.
But I went down and got a direct commission to go in the Navy.
And so as soon as I finished college, I had to go in the Navy.
And I was in the Navy and on destroyers for about,
just nearly four years was the tour date.
And the roughest duty that we had was on Okinawa.
We went up there with nine destroyers in the squadron.
And this is where the kamikazes.
And for about two or three months,
we were exposed to kamikazes every single day.
And we lost six out of our nine destroyers.
There were only three of us that made it back.
And it was just a matter of luck.
We weren't that much better.
And we just, when they'd hit one station, we'd be on another one.
Or the plane that was coming in at you, we got it shot down.
Well, then after the war, I thought about going to Georgia Tech,
but it turned out that there was no housing down there.
So I decided I'd work for Burlington Mills.
And they had put me into a little program
that was supposed to be training the plant managers of the future.
Well, it turned out that it looked like I was going to have to work
on the third shift for about 10 years, 15.
So I decided I'd just go back to graduate school.
I went back to North Carolina State.
And Dr. Lindsey had taught down there.
And so they gave me his name.
I called him and came, got in touch with him.
And so we came out here.
And I began studying the graduate program.
In chemical engineering in January of 1948.
That's it.
You came to Texas A&M?
Texas A&M in January of 1948.
And I promised my wife, because we'd been out here when I was on the destroyer,
I promised her we'd never stay in Texas.
As soon as I got my degree, we were leaving.
Well, we stayed.
Still here.
Describe what Texas A&M was physically.
Okay.
I'll give you the whole picture.
I have some.
A guy named Proctor was one of the first heads of the extension service, as I understand.
And he was my brother-in-law's mother's father.
He used to be over here in this same place of research.
He used to have a picture over there.
So we visited with the Lees on the way.
And Mr. Lee had been taught.
He'd been a high school principal.
He'd gone to the old school and studied the Latin.
He was a classical-type individual.
And he said, so he called up Bolton.
Bolton was the executive vice president.
He said, get him a room, a place to stay up there.
So after I'd already come here and gotten settled, I was over in Lindsey's office one day,
and Mr. Bolton called up and he said he wanted to see me.
Boy, they were just shaking over there.
I went over.
So he told me.
But the amusing thing is, he ended up saying, well, just go over here.
Oh, I'd forgotten.
Boyette, go see him.
He's in charge of housing.
And that was what I got out of him.
About a month or so, we were down there visiting with Mr. Lee again.
He said, well, I suppose you know Bolton well by now.
I said, I'm sorry, Mr. Lee.
Our paths haven't crossed any since that one time I met him.
But Dr. Lindsey was head of the chemical engineering department.
We were over there in the old petroleum building.
At that time, Barlow was dean of engineering.
And Saul Wright was head of civil.
Charlie Crawford was head of mechanical.
Let's see.
Shepard was dean of agriculture.
Petroleum?
No, petroleum.
Harold Vance was head of petroleum.
And Shepard was head of agriculture.
And I don't remember.
I remember Mr. Porter.
Mr. Porter was head of the math department.
But I don't know, don't remember now who was the science dean.
I think Pearson was dean of the graduate college, followed later by Trotter.
But Pearson, I believe, was the dean when I came here.
I worked most of the time, dealt with Trotter's group over there.
And I think that's, let's see, Street was teaching drawing.
And I can't remember the fellow that was head of aero.
And that's about the one.
There were no sidewalks.
It was all, actually, it was the same way it was in 1943.
They had done nothing during the war.
And what, I came in, we had already gotten over the flood here,
but there's still a lot of veterans.
And I would say that that was the toughest group to compete with I ever ran against
because all they want to know is what's required, and we'll do it.
Because you and your other veteran buddies, you were mentally, emotionally mature?
Yes.
And you were ready to go to work?
Ready to go to work.
You'd had experience?
Mm-hmm.
And also, you could separate.
I had a family, too.
Yeah.
Everything was riding on it.
What was your graduate student stipend or assistantship or what?
Oh, it was probably about $100 a month.
Right.
And plus the GI Bill.
I'm not even sure we got $100.
Maybe we did.
I don't remember what it was.
But it was, living in College View, we could make it.
But, see, by the time I got ready to finish, I ran out of there.
Oh.
I ran out of the, I took a job teaching.
I ran out of the GI Bill.
So I had to take a job teaching.
And then I had to buy a house over in College Hills.
And I bought one on Harrington Street over there.
It cost $13,700.
And that was the poorest year that I've ever experienced in my life.
My salary was $370 a month.
But Lindsay had to get special dispensation to get me $370.
And so that's kind of what it was like here then.
Now, when did you switch officially in paperwork from being a graduate student to being a faculty member?
About 52.
Okay.
So you're, all right, we're still talking about the graduate student days.
Yeah.
Well, I finished, I got my PhD in 53.
Oh, okay.
But I'd already, I took a job as instructor in 52, I think it was.
And then I believe in, I guess in 53, that fall I went ahead,
I've forgotten whether I got promoted to assistant professor.
Maybe I went a year before I got made assistant professor.
Looking back now with the clear vision of hindsight,
what courses and what teachers, instructors were the most important courses
to you?
Well, actually, we had some things back then and there that are better than they are now.
And that is that we took courses outside the department, a lot of them.
And I took mathematics, I had Luther, and I had Bayes.
And then Dr. Klippel and I, years later, we worked on,
I'd just go over with him and spend an hour a day, every once in a while,
every week we'd have a meeting and prove theorems and so on.
And chemistry, Jensen.
And Dr. Watson.
I worked under Murdock, Phil Murdock, in chemical engineering,
but Watson was one of them.
Another one that I took course over there was POTS, organic chemistry.
And Kenny Hancock was another one that I took a course under.
I also took a physics course, and Dr.
Weeks taught it.
And then after I finished my coursework, I went back and took another course
in kinetic theory under Dr. Silvey,
who goes way back.
And old Dr. Silvey, the first day, there were three of us in there,
registered for his course.
And he, two physics students and me.
And he said, he said,
Mr. Tarlin, would you like to participate in the classroom discussion?
And I said, well, I believe I'd like to do that.
Well, what that amounted to, we were presenting one-third of that course
for audit.
And then I also had there one under Dr. Klippel,
and I also had there one under Dr.
Weeks over there.
He was the first one.
I took x-rays and crystallography, and I never had vectors.
And he just, he had the course full of it,
but I learned it, picked it up from him.
And I had to compete against two graduate physics students in there, or three.
And so Jensen was an outstanding chemistry teacher.
Watson was an outstanding chemistry teacher.
In fact, all of those people,
he was never a real outstanding teacher.
He had a tremendous amount of knowledge.
But he could never finish one sentence until he started another one.
Now, what was his first name?
Because I remember Dr. Potts in agriculture.
No, this was a different Potts.
I don't remember what his first name is.
You just have to look it up.
What department?
Chemistry.
In the chemistry department.
He was the organic prof in chemistry.
Kenny Hancock taught a good course in chemistry.
All right, what about within chemical engineering?
Who were the key?
Phil Murdock, I took a course under him.
And let's see, you know,
I may have taken most all the courses I took under him.
I took one under G.L.
Fair.
He taught process control, and I took that.
But we took, in those years, we were really taking,
see, we didn't teach our own mathematics in our department.
And now all these people trying to teach everything in one department,
they never see the light of day outside the walls of their department.
Did anybody, do you remember any advice that somebody gave you
which later proved to be very valuable advice to a chemical engineer?
Oh, I don't know.
I just kind of stumbled along, I guess.
I very nearly quit shortly after, about 1955,
because I went down to Exxon and worked one summer.
And they wanted me to stay with Exxon.
And at that time,
I was making $5,000 a year,
Dr. Harris was making $5,100 a year,
and Dr. Lindsey was making $5,200 a year.
And I was an assistant professor,
and both of them were full.
And I figured, well, why should I stay here
when that's all I've got,
that's all the future I've got?
So it was, Shirley Lynch, incidentally,
in that old building was head of geology over there.
And that...
What persuaded you to stay?
Well, I just got to thinking about it
that I don't know.
What had happened was
I had to hurry up and get through
because that one year,
Phil Murdock left and Watson left
and all my committee left,
so I really pushed that to get it completed.
And I wasn't really sure whether I wanted to stay,
I guess, in that corporate structure.
You don't have near as much freedom
as you do in the industry.
What was your dissertation topic
in your area of specialization?
I'd done my master's on distillation,
and this one was on catalytic,
study of catalysts.
And so on my committee,
I had Dr. Potts from biology,
or Potter,
Potter from biology,
and Dr.
Weeks,
and Dr. Watson,
and Phil Murdock,
were the ones on it.
And I couldn't get it arranged
for the final meeting,
and I went by Watson's house one afternoon late,
and he was sitting back in the backyard
with his shirt off drinking beer.
And I told him,
I don't know if I'm going to be able to graduate this time
because I can't get this committee together.
Jordan said,
Oh, I can take care of that for you.
And he went in and called them up,
and we met over in the old
patrolling building that night.
He set it up and we all met over there,
and he said,
Do you remember any special questions
on that final oral exam?
I don't remember.
I just remember that
they asked me some question,
and I guess when you're working on some of those things
you've got so many details in your mind
and it's some little simple something
you won't see.
And they asked me that,
and then they found the weakness.
And so they just,
I don't remember what it was,
but then they just,
I think it was,
I think they just wanted to know
what the problem was with the
other, you know.
And that part I remember.
If you know it,
let's go on to the next,
you know,
but if you ever find something
that you didn't know.
As you thought about it then
and now look back,
how did our chemical engineering department,
how did our strength compare
nationally in the mid-50s?
Well, we got down
because the salaries were so poor
that at one time
we were down to just
Dr. Harris,
Dr. Lindsey, and I.
And we had to get some graduate students to help us out.
So it was all we could do to even keep the doors open.
You're saying three faculty?
We got down to three faculty.
Everybody quit because salaries,
they went to industry or somewhere
because salaries were so poor back in those days.
And that's about when I was about ready
to throw in the towel.
But then where the real change
come was when John Connolly got in there.
Salaries went up,
ours went up 30% a year.
For three or four years there.
But now let's back up.
John Calhoun came here
as dean in the 50s, didn't he?
Marlow was dean
when I came here.
And then John Calhoun came next.
Do you remember
any early impressions of Calhoun
as the dean?
I just remember that
he was much more energetic than
well,
Marlow was energetic enough
except that I thought Calhoun
was a much better individual
than Marlow was.
Did Calhoun
did he come over and visit?
Did he become acquainted with you all?
Oh yeah, he had us over.
He had me over to his office.
And I think he was kind of leery of old Dr. Lindsey.
And
Doc had already told him, Calhoun told me
he says, well here's my
territory and over there's yours.
Don't you get in here.
Don't you come across this.
But he did ask me one time.
He had me in his office talking.
I think I irritated him because
he said to me, he said
Calhoun.
That's Calhoun.
He wanted to know
any disincentives
or negative
things about Lindsey.
I said, well even if I had some
I wouldn't tell you.
So that ended that discussion.
I may have hurt his feelings but
I was pretty loyal to him.
Old Doc did the best he could
with what he had and he didn't have much to work with.
Now the enrollment I'm assuming
in engineering in the 50's
was going up.
Well no, actually what happened was
the enrollment of everybody went down to about 1955
which was a low point.
It was after the war was over
and this was about in there somewhere
maybe even after that they decided that
they wanted to make it optional corps
a little bit.
And I think we went on that program for a little while
and enrollment started going up.
Then we went back to compulsory corps
and it started going down again.
So the enrollment never
you could just do everything you wanted to
and you couldn't end up with much more than two sections
of about 20 some each.
And you all still had the graduate program.
Oh yeah.
During those dry years.
And I presume that industry
was generally hiring the bright
youngsters when they graduated
Yeah, we had
they were
we were always able to place them
and we helped place all those people.
The only year that I couldn't get them placed
was I believe in about 1980
when enrollment
had just gone out of proportion
and at the same time
the job market dropped completely
so we had a real problem then.
Alright.
But then Benson was after Calhoun
Calhoun really wasn't
Dean very long.
He was up to what?
Was he a system?
He went up to vice presidential
vice president for engineering I think.
Right.
When did you first meet Fred Benson?
Well I met him
before he became Dean.
I knew him because we were both in a naval
reserve unit together.
And he wasn't very
and we actually were taking one of the math courses
there together at one time.
He was taking one course, a lower one than I was taking.
Did he do his degree here?
Yeah he got his
B.S.
at Kansas
and then he got a master's several years
back at here
as I recall
and then he was taking some additional courses.
Describe Fred Benson
as a human being, as a personality.
Well
we got along just fine.
One of the things when I got appointed
what he did was call me in one afternoon
and he said Charlie
I'd like for you to take over here as head of the department.
I said now you don't have to tell me
right now.
You can wait until a little later and tell me
if you think it over.
And so I did
and I wasn't really sure I wanted to.
I really wanted to tell him
how about getting old Doc to take it one more year.
I'd like to do the things I'm doing a little longer.
But I decided to go ahead
and take it because I thought that
I could probably do more things
that I wanted to do if I were head
than I could if they got somebody from the outside
and he started giving me all kinds of directions.
When was that?
What year?
That was in 1964.
At that point
you said things you wanted to do.
What were your goals?
I'd gone to Exxon in 55
and gotten started
modeling
doing distillation on computers
and actually setting up programs
to solve problems on them.
And I worked with them, consulted with them
and I had graduate students.
We'd try out new methods
and we had an old 650 and that's what they had
at Exxon.
And we had an old 650 and they put it up
in the old EE building.
Now this is an old key punch?
Yeah, in the building.
And my graduate students would go up there
and use it at night and it had a fire escape
so they'd go up to the fire escape
and run that thing all night long.
That's what we did.
That's the way we got our time.
And we had a lot of that going on.
I'd written a book and a good many papers on it.
Methods that we had devised
to solve these problems.
So this was, at this point
primarily petro-chemically related.
Yeah.
Or you can do chemical either one.
It was,
it's used in both fields.
Not just one.
But
back to Benson.
Describe him.
What did he like and not like?
How did he operate?
Well, he
I think although he would claim
that he wanted everybody to say what they wanted to
he really wanted to hear
the right words.
And he had around him people
that he knew would always back him up.
I remember I used to go in there
to budget hearings.
And he'd have
Ed Fenner.
Then he'd have Old Bain, Spencer
and he'd have two or three others like that.
And he'd say something
and they'd all chime in.
But
he and I got along pretty well
because I never did
I never did go in and ask him
any questions for me.
I went ahead and solved the things.
The only time he and I got crosswise
was he was pushing real hard
for this Doctor of Engineering program.
And he had all of us
department heads in there one day.
And what he was wanting us to do
was agree not to
weaken down the B.S.
degree
to where it would not be an accredited degree
and have the Doctor of Engineering
be the first accredited degree.
And I stood up against him
because I told him that we could not
afford to have a large group
of students coming out of here
without an accredited degree.
If we were going to give a B.S.
degree
we'd better make it accredited.
And he got so mad at me
that he walked out
and stayed gone for about 15 minutes.
And then we finally
decided we'd have it
kind of optional.
We'd always have the B.S.
accredited.
But if they wanted to go at an early stage
somewhere over to the Doctor of Engineering
they could.
And so we'd just leave it
where whichever one won out
would be the way we'd go.
And I told them we were just not big enough
to fight against the whole United States
on one program like that.
They'd run us out of business.
So he and I
and then later he says Charlie
you can do whatever you want to.
But he told me many times
that I had the best department in college engineering.
How long
in years was that Doctor of Engineering
degree planned to be?
Oh it was slightly longer I think
than a Masters.
And it was made up of course work plus
a problem in industry.
It was actually a real good degree.
It might fit engineering better
than some of the real
because when you get over too far in the research
you're nearly like a chemist or a physicist.
And
you could make a good program
out of that where you would actually do research
on things that pertain to engineering
per se more than
you can on some of the
directions they're going in because they get
toward the science end
of it and then you're dealing with the physicist
or the chemist and so on.
So it was a good degree.
It was just that I didn't think that we could swing it.
What did happen to it?
Well there's still
I think they still give some of them
but it just wasn't
the demand for it.
It's just hard to sell.
So there was a key decision
that I had something to do.
I saved him there really.
In Benson's personality
did he laugh much?
No he was always just
in the meetings there was never
much said.
He got to the point pretty quickly
didn't he?
Oh yeah.
And it was
well thought out.
Anything he did was well thought out.
He was good to work for.
I didn't have a bit of trouble working for him.
We just had
I mean he knew that I wasn't back down anymore.
I'd express
my opinion.
He didn't like it
but
he respected it and then he
told me later I think he really
it hurt him that he couldn't handle it better
than he did.
In your early
years as department head
who were your key
associates
as department heads
elsewhere in engineering
and anywhere else?
Oh well actually
in those years I knew all the
department heads real well.
But who were the
ones you worked with most?
Well I knew Semang real well.
He and I were well acquainted.
Describe him.
Well
he was very precise
and very fair
and I had a lot of
respect for him and he
did for me.
Describe him physically.
